title: The One On The Last Night

content.problemStatement:
Chandler wants to give Joey money as Joey is going through some financial struggles. He believes $1500 will be enough to pay off the bills. Chandler makes up a game on the spot called "Cups", which has no rules. Every move Joey makes in the "game", Chandler says that he wins a lot of money. Joey, being "smart", finds this out and thus wants to set some rules.

Joey and Chandler have a set of cards, where the **value** of a card is defined as the product of all the digits of the number printed on that card.

For example, value of the card with the number 777 equals 777, value of the card with the number 203203203 equals 2⋅0⋅3=02 \cdot 0 \cdot 3 = 02⋅0⋅3=0.

The initial number on Joey's card is NNN (without any leading zeroes). Chandler can make at most KKK changes on the digits of the number before it gets noticed. In one change, he can choose any digit xxx (x≠9x ≠ 9x=9) of the number printed on card and replace it with x+1x+1x+1.
Find the maximum possible value of Joey's card after making the changes.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
For each test case, given two integer NNN and KKK, denoting the initial number on Joey's card and the number of changes Chandler can make.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line the maximum possible value of Joey's card after making the changes.
constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 10^6 (so the length of N is at most 7)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a string N (the initial number on the card, without leading zeros) and an integer K separated by a space.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible value of the card after at most K digit‑increment operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: increase the single digit 1 five times → 6, product = 6.\nTest case 2: digits 2,2,2,1. Increment 1→2, then two of the 2's →3, final digits 3,3,2,2, product = 36.\nTest case 3: no changes, product of 1·2·3·4·5·6 = 720.

hidden.label: edge case with zeros, insufficient K

solution.approach: The product of the digits is maximized by always increasing the smallest digit that is still less than 9. Each increment changes a digit d to d+1 and multiplies the total product by (d+1)/d, which is larger for smaller d. Therefore we repeatedly pick the current minimum digit, increase it by one, and continue until we run out of operations or all digits become 9. A min‑heap (priority queue) stores the digits, allowing O(log L) time per increment where L is the number of digits (L ≤ 7). After all operations the product of the digits is computed. Special care is taken for zeros: because a zero makes the whole product zero, the greedy rule naturally upgrades zeros first, turning the product from zero to a positive value as soon as the first zero is increased.
solution.timeComplexity: O((L + K) · log L) per test case, where L ≤ 7. In practice this is O(K) because L is tiny.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(L) for the heap storing the digits.

code.Python3:
import sys, heapq

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it)
        K = int(next(it))
        heap = [int(ch) for ch in N]
        heapq.heapify(heap)
        while K > 0 and heap[0] < 9:
            d = heapq.heappop(heap)
            heapq.heappush(heap, d + 1)
            K -= 1
        prod = 1
        for d in heap:
            prod *= d
        out_lines.append(str(prod))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
